narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 41% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for admission. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your second-decile rankings in both first-year and third-year school grades—meaning you were in the lower 20% of your peers—alongside a middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 35, a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.7, being a female applicant, White racial background, and planning full-time enrollment. While your undergrad GPA stands out as stronger than average, the model weighed several key factors more heavily in pushing toward this failure risk. The biggest influence came from your third-year decile ranking, which significantly raised the predicted risk compared to students who ranked higher in their classes. Right behind that was your first-year decile, similarly signaling early academic struggles relative to typical applicants. Your LSAT score followed closely, as it fell a bit short of the average, nudging the prediction further toward failure. Family income played a notable role too, with your middle bracket contributing less favorably than higher ones often do. Other aspects like your race, gender, and full-time plans had smaller impacts but still tilted slightly against you in this case. In contrast, your strong undergrad GPA helped a little by pulling the odds toward passing, though not enough to offset the top concerns. Overall, your profile shows lower early-law-school performance and test scores compared to peers who typically get approved, putting you at higher risk in the model's eyes. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: consider retaking the LSAT to aim above the average of 36.7, build even stronger academic habits to improve any ongoing grade deciles, and highlight ways to leverage your excellent undergrad record in reapplying. With targeted prep for the bar itself, like dedicated study strategies, you could shift this prediction dramatically. Keep pushing—you've already shown GPA strength that many lack, and persistence pays off for students in similar spots.
